title: Fermions are fundamentally more nonlocal than Bosons
authors: Fatemeh Moradi Kalarde, Sadra Boreiri, Xiangling Xu, Lucas Tendick, Salman Beigi, Paolo Perinotti, Tommaso Guaita, Marc-Olivier Renou
abstract: Bell's theorem shows that entangled quantum particles can exhibit correlations that classical particles cannot reproduce without an additional nonlocal resource, such as communication. In this sense, quantum particles are fundamentally more nonlocal than classical ones, and entanglement becomes unavoidable in physics. Here we prove the analogous result within quantum theory itself: indistinguishable fermions transmitted through a quantum network can generate correlations that distinguishable particles or indistinguishable bosons cannot reproduce without additional communication. In the same sense, fermions are fundamentally more nonlocal than bosons or distinguishable particles, motivating fermionic anticommutation and indistinguishability as unavoidable operational resources. Our result further implies that fermions can strictly surpass all qubit-based protocols for certain distributed computing tasks, demonstrating that a complete understanding of information processing requires going beyond qubits to fermionic information carriers - febits.
summary: This paper proves that indistinguishable fermions possess a unique, stronger form of nonlocality compared to bosons or qubits. By analyzing multi-party network correlations, the authors demonstrate that fermionic anticommutation is an unavoidable operational resource necessary for certain quantum information tasks. This work advocates for developing 'febits' as essential carriers beyond standard qubit descriptions.
main_problem: To prove that indistinguishable fermions generate correlations fundamentally stronger than those achievable by bosons or distinguishable particles, thereby establishing fermionic nonlocality as a key resource.
main_result: Fermions are shown to be fundamentally more nonlocal than bosons or qubits, implying that fermionic information carriers (febits) can surpass qubit-based protocols for certain distributed computing tasks.
method: The analysis uses analogies to Bell's theorem and constructs multi-party 'Gedankenexperiments' involving local communication rounds in a network setting. The core technique involves comparing conditional probability distributions derived from fermionic anticommutation versus bosonic/qubit models.
model_or_system: The model compares three types of quantum carriers: qubits (internal states), indistinguishable bosons, and indistinguishable fermions. The physics is analyzed using second quantization formalism in the context of distributed quantum communication networks or multi-party loops.
key_observables: Exchange phase ($\chi = -1$ for fermions vs $\chi = 1$ for bosons); conditional probability distributions $P_{f}$ vs $P_{	ext{Q-QIT}}$; total variation distance ($d_{	ext{cond}}^{	ext{TV}}$).
important_parameters: Loop size $N$; number of communication rounds; the parity of fermionic exchanges.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a locality constraint where local operations cannot depend on an unknown global network configuration ($\ell$), and that the comparison is restricted to single-round or limited-round communication protocols.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate cyclic exchange showing phase differences; another figure depicts a 'Distributed rewiring game' comparing fermionic vs. qubit simulation outcomes across various hidden wirings.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the fundamental difference in correlation generation (fermions vs bosons) to analyzing multi-party loop structures, culminating in detailed mathematical proofs regarding operator identities and structural properties of unitaries acting on tensor product spaces.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous theoretical framework for understanding how particle statistics (Pauli exclusion principle) translate into nonclassical resources for quantum computation and communication protocols beyond standard qubit models.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Cyclic exchange and fermionic phase. (a) N parties A1, . . . , AN initially hold one particle (represented by the black disk) each, in a global state |ψ0⟩= a† 1 · · · a† N|vac⟩. a† i denotes the bosonic or fermionic creation operator of particle at mode i, depending on the particle type. (b) After a clockwise cyclic shift of the particles, the state becomes |ψ1⟩= a† Na† 1 · · · a† N−1|vac⟩= χN−1|ψ0⟩. For bosons, χ = 1, while for fermions χ = −1, giving a parity-dependent global phase.

Figure 2: Distributed rewiring game: (a) Parties A, B, C each locally prepare bipartite systems, keep one subsystem, and send the other to a referee R. The referee is a passive relay introduced to formalize the requirement that the local operations of the parties do not depend on the global network configuration. The referee secretly chooses a wiring l ∈{ABC, ACB, AB, AC, BC} and redistributes the sent subsystems accordingly as illustrated in (b), while also assigning inputs xi ∈{0, 1}. Following the protocol described in the main text, each party i ∈{A, B, C} performs an input-dependent measurement and obtains an outcome ai ∈{0, 1+, 1−, 2}.
